title: Italic swash capitals, the formal paired majuscules
subtitle: The flourished capitals written with a broad-edge nib at the Italic pen angle
excerpt: Italic swash capitals are the companion majuscules for the Italic chancery hand. They slope at 5-10° like the minuscule and are written at 7-8 nib-widths height. The swash is a single elongated curve that begins the capital: an entry flourish that distinguishes the formal Italic hand from the compressed Italic business hand.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), public domain, Internet Archive, chapter on italic and humanist capitals. Johnston's italic capital analysis describes the swash entry as a structural feature, not a decoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: An elongated, curved entry or exit flourish on a capital letter. In Italic calligraphy, the swash is the defining feature of the formal capitals, distinguishing them from the plain compressed Italic capitals of business use.

content.text: Italic chancellery script uses two capital styles. The plain compressed capital is for functional documents. The

capital is for display headings, addressed letters, and formal calligraphy. The swash is not a florid addition. It is the opening movement of the letter: a longer version of the entry stroke that begins the main stem.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: Italic swash capitals are written at 45° pen angle, 7-8 nib-widths height. A size 3 nib gives a comfortable cap height of around 10 mm.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm (slug: cartridge-paper-220)
items.description: Heavy enough for ink without bleed. Rule guidelines at 7-8 nib-widths for the cap height.

content.text: Hold the nib at 45° from the horizontal: the standard Italic angle. The capital slopes at the same 5-10° as the Italic minuscule. Keep the slope consistent: a straight-legged A and a sloped B on the same page look like different hands. The swash does not slope; it is a horizontal or slightly upward curve that transitions into the sloped main stroke.

The swash begins to the left of the letter, at cap height or slightly above. It moves rightward as a thin curved stroke (hairline at 45° angle because the stroke direction is near-parallel to the pen edge), curves down and inward to form the entry into the main stem. The transition from swash curve to main stem is the technical challenge: the pen angle does not change, but the stroke direction rotates from horizontal to vertical, so the stroke widens as the direction changes.

Practise the swash-into-stem transition in isolation: a thin curved stroke moving right, rotating to downward, producing a consistent thickening at the rotation point. Fill a ruled line with this curve before attempting any letter.

A: swash curves in from the left at cap height and becomes the left diagonal; a second diagonal from the right side; crossbar. B: swash enters the head of the left stem; the stem descends, two bumps on the right. C: the entire opening curve of C can be treated as a swash; it starts with a thin curved entry, then the curve deepens and widens. D: swash as the stem entry. E: swash entry on the top arm, then the three arms. F: swash entry on the top arm. G: swash entry on the curved portion. H: swash on the left stem head; the right stem has a plain serif. I: a horizontal top swash followed by the vertical stroke. K: swash entry on the stem; the two diagonal branches are plain. L: swash entry on the head of the vertical; plain foot. M and N: swash on the outer left stem head.

Not every capital has a swash in every tradition. The formal rule from Johnston: use swashes consistently within a piece, not on some letters and not others. A single swash A on an otherwise plain page reads as a mistake; a consistent swash alphabet reads as a deliberate choice.
